name: callbacker
documentation:
# Callbacker

Callbacker is a lightweight and flexible callback library for both synchronous and asynchronous Python programming. It provides a simple yet powerful way to implement event-driven programming patterns in Python.

## Features

- Support for both synchronous and asynchronous callbacks
- Decorator-based event registration
- Type-safe event handling
- Easy to integrate with existing codebases
- Minimal dependencies (only requires `frozenlist`)
- Python 3.11+ support
- Thread-safe event dispatching

## Installation

You can install Callbacker using PDM:

```sh
pip callbacker
```

## Quick Start

Here's a simple example of how to use Callbacker:

```python
from callbacker.callback import EventWrapper

# Create an event
custom_event = EventWrapper()

# Register a callback using decorator syntax
@custom_event
async def on_event(data):
    print(f"Received event with data: {data}")

# Trigger the event asynchronously
await custom_event.aio_send("Hello, World!")
```

## Advanced Usage

### Class-based Events

```python
from callbacker.callback import event, contextevent

class MyClass:
    @event
    async def on_data(self, data):
        """This is a regular event"""
        print(f"Received data: {data}")

    @contextevent
    async def on_update(self, context, data):
        """This event includes the class instance as context"""
        print(f"Update from {context} with data: {data}")
```

### Synchronous Callbacks

```python
from callbacker.callback import EventWrapper

event = EventWrapper()

@event
def sync_handler(data):
    print(f"Handling {data} synchronously")

# Trigger synchronously
event.send("test data")
```

## Documentation

For more detailed documentation and examples, please visit [the documentation page](https://github.com/ipfans/callbacker).

## Contributing

Contributions are welcome! Please read our [Contributing Guidelines](CONTRIBUTING.md) for details on how to submit pull requests, report issues, and contribute to the project.

## License

classes.name: callbacker.signal.Signal

methods.name: callbacker.signal.Signal.send
documentation: Sends data to all registered receivers. It will skip async receivers.

methods.name: callbacker.signal.Signal.aio_send
documentation: Sends data to all registered receivers.

Coroutine-based signal implementation.

To connect a callback to a signal, use any list method.

Signals are fired using the send() for sync code and aio_send for coroutine,
which takes named arguments.

functions.name: callbacker.callback.subclassevent
documentation: Turns off abstract functions allowing inner functions to be events

functions.name: callbacker.callback.subclasscontextevent
documentation: Turns off abstract functions allowing inner functions to be events

classes.name: callbacker.callback.EventWrapper

methods.name: callbacker.callback.EventWrapper.__call__
documentation:
appends a callback function to the event, returns the same function for futher use elsewhere...

: this is equivilent to calling the `append()` method

```
from callbacker.callback import EventWrapper

custom_event = EventWrapper()

@custom_event
async def on_event():
    ...
```
A wrapper class for making a callback function that carries a few more methods than aiosignal has.

classes.name: callbacker.callback.SelfEventWrapper
documentation: A wrapper class for making a callback that carries itself for use with each callback...

classes.name: callbacker.callback.event
documentation:
A Couroutine Based implementation of an asynchronous callback object. 
This object is a replacement for aiosignal. with easier configuration options...

abstract: `bool` inner function upon being called is considered abstract...         if `true` inner custom function will not be called with the `send()` method and it         will be considered as nothing but typehinting.
"""Freezes all events for a base context object for making events easier to serlize
before beginning to use the inner signals for different callbacks"""

classes.name: callbacker.callback.contextevent
documentation:
An event who's callback Carries the class object as a context to be used with the event...

abstract: `bool` inner function upon being called is considered abstract...         if `true` inner custom function will not be called with the `send()` method and it         will be considered as nothing but typehinting.
